text: as ever. She seethed. She wept. Then, with phone in hand, she plotted. # “There have to be no other options.” Lisa confirms the cancellation of her cell service, salivating over her ingenuity. “I have to be helpless,” she says as she snips the cafe’s landline. “I have to be hopeless,” she says as she snips her car’s fuel line. It seemed so easy weeks ago. All she needed was a clean video of Frankie performing his miracle. But paper-cuts and bruised elbows weren’t going to do the job, she quickly discovered, and with her phone at the ready, Lisa ramped up her attempts at coaxing out Frankie’s magic. It wasn’t the first time she held a knife to her arm. Nor was it the first time she dared herself to press a little harder. It wasn’t even her first time with that particular knife. In hindsight, she should’ve known it wouldn’t work. Something she did to make Charlie understand the depths of her misery could never inspire something miraculous, especially since she hadn’t even wanted to get Charlie’s attention like that. Some people were just that dense. You could cry and scream and starve yourself, and they still wouldn’t see your suffering until you opened a vein on their church shoes. His face that day was a soft sheet of white like pizza dough hanging off his skull: an expression she's seen for years after the fact. But never straight-on. His face gapes in her periphery, sometimes so real she felt like he’s spent the last few years hiding in her blind spot. The blind spot is much wider now. As she pierced her forearm with the boning knife, as she shredded her fist punching through drywall, and as she held her arm over the stove-top flame so long her flesh bubbled and burst, leaving skin splatters on the burner, she saw Charlie, still wrapped up, sometimes dead, sometimes alive, but always laughing in time with the studio audience. Lisa did terrible things to others for the sake of her dream, and now it was her turn. But no matter how many slabs of flesh she sliced away, Frankie wouldn’t heal her. Sometimes he’d stand nearby, licking his ass while she bled. Sometimes he wouldn’t show up at all. But the shadowy audience was always there, applauding her gumption. And why wouldn’t they? What was skin or fingers or ears compared to the future bought with Frankie’s magic? His Instagram post had just been a picture and a few descriptors. Imagine the response to a video of the beast curing cancer. Now, as she waits for the cat to arrive, it's all she can think about. All the comments. All the shares. All the love. She fantasizes about popularity the way she fantasized about the cafe, knowing that manifestation requires a bit of obsession. It was no simple thing bringing a dream to life, let alone sustaining it. One had to surround themselves with like-minded dreamers in order to keep the passion alight. She had quite a bit of that during business hours, but once she was back in the house with Charlie’s dreamless aura, she felt a strange sort of doom strangling her. She couldn’t breathe sometimes when he was around. She tried to convince herself it was just anxiety, not a sign that sustaining was out of her grasp. After all, she’d done it well enough in other aspects: the lives of dozens of cats and house plants, for instance. She also had a knack for sustaining secrets. As did the shadows, some of which kept the same secrets as she, because they had no choice. If only she’d been a window he could look out in perfect contentment. But she’s so much more. She’s the glass and the landscape and the angry sky beyond it all. She’s the house too, the cafe and the dirt beneath, and every single mushroom sprouting in the cellar. Splayed in the foyer once again, kissing air and calling Frankie’s name, her gaze rolls over the ceiling: the tiny cracks Charlie always meant to fix, the flecks of gold contradicting them, the beam of rainbow sunlight bending over a dusty security camera— “Oh my God...” The audience claps, and Lisa’s breath catches in her throat, followed by a glut of blood she coughs up on her blouse. It rolls down her chest and joins the large bloodstain on her weeping stomach as she pushes herself up. Fighting the shutdown had occupied her so completely she’d neglected to turn off the security cameras when she closed down the cafe. If she was lucky, they’d been recording and downloading to her laptop all this time, months of the bistro and house from multiple angles, inside and out. All she had to do was find one good recording of Frankie's miracle. She doesn’t know which organ she’s holding inside as she struggles to stand. But it won’t matter soon. She just needs to get to her laptop upstairs. Something heavy and dark as shame pursues her through the house like an injured animal dragging its mangled legs. Though she never sees it, she smells where it's been, the stench of rot greeting her around every corner. Its shadow is more like an ink stain smeared on the wall than a kite tied to a living thing. It doesn’t move in time with the thing following her to the bedroom, but it crowds around her all the same as she opens her laptop and smears blood over the touchscreen. The cats crowd her too, yowling with a hunger for which Lisa deserves the blame. “Just a minute,” she tells them, breathless. “I just need to do this one thing and I’ll feed you.” They’ve heard it a lot over the last few weeks and no longer believe her. But she’s hungry too, and when they sniff her wounds, she doesn’t have the energy to wave them away. They lick and tug at the dangling meat
